result:
The rain is a soft percussion on the roof, a sound so steady it becomes a kind of silence. It’s in this hushed, grey-lit space that my mind tends to wander, unspooling like a reel of film left in the sun—warped, bleached in places, startlingly vivid in others.

Lately, I’ve been thinking about abandoned places. Not the grand, tragic ruins of castles or theaters, but the small, intimate abandonments. The coffee mug left on a park bench, the imprint of a body on a couch cushion after someone has risen, the single child’s glove dangling from a fence. These are the ghosts of a recent past, still warm with intention. Whoever left that mug thought, “I’ll just set this here for a moment,” and then their life carried them away, and the moment became permanent. There’s a strange democracy to lost objects. They belong to no one and to the weather now.

This thought connects, as thoughts do in the rain, to a memory of my grandmother’s hands. They were always in motion: kneading dough, tracing the lines of a well-read Bible, pulling weeds from her fierce little rose garden. Her hands were a map of her life—veins like rivers, knuckles swollen continents, brown spots like islands. When she died, her hands became still. And all that motion, all that intention and love and irritation channeled through those hands… where did it go? Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed. So did the force of her kneading become the lift of a sparrow’s wing? Did her soothing pats on my back turn into the vibration of a cricket’s song in the late summer grass? I like to think so. I like to think we don’t vanish; we just become less concentrated, distributed back into the hum of things.

It makes me look at my own hands, typing this. What maps are they drawing? What tiny, unconscious abandonments am I creating right now? The heat of my fingertips on the keys, the exhalation of my breath fogging the dark window—these are my ephemeral contributions to the atmosphere. We are all slowly evaporating, leaving behind a trail of shed skin cells, warmth, and carbon dioxide, becoming part of someone else’s inhaled moment.

The rain is slowing now. The percussion fades to a drip-drip on the downspout. The world outside the window is washed clean, glittering under a breach of sunlight that has found its way through the cloud armor. Everything looks new, but it’s all made of old things. The water in that puddle has been in oceans, in clouds, in the roots of giant sequoias, in the tears of countless beings. It has been abandoned and collected again and again.

And maybe that’s the thread: we are all temporary collections of stardust and story, in the process of being gently, constantly, abandoned and gathered up. We leave our gloves on fences. We leave our warmth in empty chairs. And the universe, in its slow, patient economy, uses those abandoned bits to build the next moment of beauty—a rose, a sparrow, a child’s laugh, a patch of sunlight on a wet street.

The thought isn’t sad. It’s a deep, quiet comfort. It means we are never truly gone. We are just rearranged. We are the mug on the bench, waiting for the rain to fill us up with a different sky.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
